coredata.dc:title: A sensitive electrochemiluminescence biosensor for assay of cancer biomarker (MMP-2) based on NGQDs-Ru@SiO2 luminophore
coredata.dc:creator: Fan, Xuemei
Wang, Shumin
Liu, Hugang
Li, Zhejian
Sun, Qiangqiang
Wang, Yimeng
Fan, Xinhui
coredata.dc:description: A sensitive biosensor that can be used for the determination of matrix metalloproteinase 2 (MMP-2) was proposed. The biosensor was developed by using an excellent self-enhanced nanocomposites as an illuminant and a peptide as a recognition element. For the electrostatic attraction between Ru(bpy)3 2+ and nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (NGQDs), the self-enhanced electrochemiluminescence (ECL) nanocomposites of NGQDs-Ru(bpy)3 2+-doped silica nanoparticles (NGQDs-Ru@SiO2) were synthesized through a simple sol-gel process. Then, a specific peptide (labeled sulfhydryl) was combined with the self-enhanced ECL nanocomposites (carboxyl in NGQDs) via acylation reaction to obtain the peptide-NGQDs-Ru@SiO2 nanoprobe, which was fabricated onto the gold electrode surface via Au–S bond. The peptide of the ECL nanoprobe was exposed to cleavage in the presence of MMP-2, which caused the signal substance to move farther away from the electrode, leading to a decrease of the ECL signal. The proposed NGQDs-Ru@SiO2-labeled peptide ECL biosensor displayed a lower detection limit of 6.5 pg mL−1 than those of reported ECL methods. The proposed biosensor provided an outlook for future applications in other disease-associated biomarkers.
coredata.dcterms:subject: NGQDs-Ru@SiO2 self-enhanced nanocomposites
Peptide
Matrix metalloproteinase 2

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Characterization of ECL biosensor and feasibility study for MMP-2 detection

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: SEM image (A), Elemental mapping (B), TEM image (C), fluorescence spectra (D), Zeta potential (E), FT-IR spectra (F), and XPS spectra of NGQDs-Ru@SiO2 (G). Inset: photos of NGQDs-Ru@SiO2 (D).

The ECL intensity-potential profiles in 0.1 mol L−1 PBS (pH = 7.0) containing 50 mmol L−1 TPA. A: Ru(bpy)3 2+ (a), a + GQDs (b), a + NGQDs (c), B: NGQDs (a), Ru@SiO2 (b), NGQDs + Ru@SiO2 (c), NGQDs-Ru@SiO2 (d). high voltage: 800 V.

CV (A) and EIS (B) curves at 0.05 mol L−1 [Fe(CN)6]3-/4 (0.1 mol L−1 KCl) and ECL response at 0.1 mol L−1 PBS (pH = 7.0) containing 50 mmol L−1 TPA (C). Bare gold electrode (a), peptide-NGQDs-Ru@SiO2 modified gold electrode (b), peptide-NGQDs-Ru@SiO2/MCH modified gold electrode (c), peptide-NGQDs-Ru@SiO2/MCH modified gold electrodes with 10 ng mL −1(d), and 150 ng mL −1 of MMP-2 (e). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

ECL responses to MMP-2 at different concentrations (A). a to h: 0, 0.01, 10, 45, 80, 115, 150, and 185 ng mL−1. Inset: The calibration curve of MMP-2. Selectivity of the ECL biosensor with various interferences (B). Repeatability (blue bar charts of C) and stability of the. ECL biosensor for MMP-2 (red bar charts of C). MMP-2: 10 ng mL−1. . (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
Schematic illustration of the synthesis of nanoprobe and fabrication of the MMP-2 ECL biosensor.
Performance comparison of several biosensors for MMP-2.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: FL: fluorescence, EC: electrochemistry, ECL: electrochemiluminescence.

head.ce:title: A sensitive electrochemiluminescence biosensor for assay of cancer biomarker (MMP-2) based on NGQDs-Ru@SiO2 luminophore

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials Science and Chemical Engineering, Xi'an Technological University, Xi'an, 710021, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Chemical Engineering

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemical Engineering and Modern Materials

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. School of Materials science and Chemical Engineering, Xi'an Technological University, Xi'an, 710021, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials science and Chemical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A sensitive biosensor that can be used for the determination of matrix metalloproteinase 2 (MMP-2) was proposed. The biosensor was developed by using an excellent self-enhanced nanocomposites as an illuminant and a peptide as a recognition element. For the electrostatic attraction between Ru(bpy)3 2+ and nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (NGQDs), the self-enhanced electrochemiluminescence (ECL) nanocomposites of NGQDs-Ru(bpy)3 2+-doped silica nanoparticles (NGQDs-Ru@SiO2) were synthesized through a simple sol-gel process. Then, a specific peptide (labeled sulfhydryl) was combined with the self-enhanced ECL nanocomposites (carboxyl in NGQDs) via acylation reaction to obtain the peptide-NGQDs-Ru@SiO2 nanoprobe, which was fabricated onto the gold electrode surface via Au–S bond. The peptide of the ECL nanoprobe was exposed to cleavage in the presence of MMP-2, which caused the signal substance to move farther away from the electrode, leading to a decrease of the ECL signal. The proposed NGQDs-Ru@SiO2-labeled peptide ECL biosensor displayed a lower detection limit of 6.5 pg mL−1 than those of reported ECL methods. The proposed biosensor provided an outlook for future applications in other disease-associated biomarkers.

ce:section.ce:para: Cancer is a major killer of humans, and its early detection can greatly improve patients' survival rates. Matrix metalloproteinases (MMPs) are a family of proteases, and many MMPs are useful biomarkers for cancer [1,2]. Among them, MMP-2 is the most vital biomarker of breast cancer, bladder cancer and cervical cancers [3,4]. Therefore, a highly selective and sensitive detection method for MMP-2 is the key to monitoring and evaluating curative efficacy. Detection results can be used as the basis for differentiating the benign and malignant tumors. Traditional clinical diagnostic methods include positron emission tomography-computed tomography (PET-CT), X-ray photography and endoscopy. However, PET-CT is harmful to humans, because the use of radiation and X-ray is not suitable for early screening. Endoscopy is an invasive examination and can easily cause many complications.

Biomarkers contribute to the early diagnosis of cancer and can be extracted from blood, urine, and saliva. The principle of biosensors mainly relies on the conformational changes caused by the combination of the analyte or the association/dissociation of the recognition molecules–analyte complex, resulting a change in signal. Some biosensors have been developed for MMP-2 detection, including immunoassay [5], zymography [6], fluorescence [7–9], and electrochemical approaches [10–12]. Over the last decades, electrochemiluminescence (ECL) has been a great success for its simple equipment, near-zero background, good electrochemical performance and excellent sensitivity [13]. At present, few ECL biosensors for detecting MMP-2 have been reported. Dang et al. developed a method to monitor MMP-2 by using peptide as a molecular recognition tool with a detection limit of 0.7 ng mL−1 [14]. Gao et al. utilized Ir1 and Ru1 complex, which have large different ECL peak potentials; they fabricated a multiplexed biosensor for MMP-2 and MMP-7 with a detection limit of 5.0 ng mL−1 for MMP-2 [15]. Although some achievements have been made, establishing a more sensitive method to detect MMP-2 is still necessary.

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs), which belong to a class of “zero-dimensional” carbon materials characterized by an oxygenous surface, have increasingly become a research trend in ECL biological sensing for their good water solubility, low toxicity, biocompatibility and photostability [16]. GQDs could act as co-reactant to catalyze or amplify the most popular ECL reagents, such as Ru(bpy)3 2+ [17–22] and luminol [23–25]. The ECL intensity depends largely on the efficiency of the electron transfer between illuminant and co-reactant [26]. The intramolecular self-enhancing ECL emitter can contract the electron transfer distance and depreciate energy loss by covalent means [27]. For the sake of further improving luminous efficiency, lots of self-enhanced ECL composites, such as Ru(bpy)2(phen-NH2)2+-NCQDs [28], Ru(dcbpy)3 2+-NCQDs [29], CdS/Au@SiO2@RuDS [30], PEI- Nss-TiO2@Ru(bpy)3 2+-Bi NBs [31], Ni-TCPP (Fe)-PEI- luminol [32], CuMn–CeO2-PEI-luminol [33], TiO2 MCs@rGO)/DBAE luminol [34], and Au–Pd–Pt/MoS2-cys [35], have been prepared. Some GQD-based self-enhanced ECL biosensors have been established and applied to drug analysis [36], environmental detection [37], food [38], and biological analysis [39]. However, the development of self-enhanced ECL luminescent material for MMP-2 detection based on nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (NGQDs) as co-reactant has not been reported.

Here, a novel sensitive ECL biosensor for MMP-2 was constructed. First, an intramolecular self-enhanced nanocomposites of NGQDs-Ru@SiO2 was prepared based on NGQDs which can be used as a co-reactant of Ru(bpy)3 2+. Owing to the short distance between illuminant and co-reactant, NGQDs-Ru@SiO2 showed strong ECL properties. Then, a specific peptide (labeled with sulfhydryl) combined with the ECL self-enhanced nanocomposites (carboxyl in NGQDs) via acylation reaction to form the peptide-NGQDs-Ru@SiO2 nanoprobe. Subsequently, the ECL nanoprobe was fabricated onto the gold electrode surface via Au–S bond. Ultimately, with the existence of the target molecule MMP-2, the peptide of the ECL nanoprobe was shown to have specific cleavage [40], which caused part of peptide-NGQDs-Ru@SiO2 to leave the electrode surface, thereby resulting in the decrease of the ECL signal. The proposed NGQDs-Ru@SiO2-labeled peptide ECL biosensor exhibited a lower detection limit of 6.5 pg mL−1. The developed biosensor was applied to the detection of MMP-2 in serum sample, thereby demonstrating the advantage of painless clinical analysis. We compared our method for NGQDs-Ru@SiO2 synthesis with similar research works [37,38]. In our experiment, we directly synthesized NGQDs-Ru@SiO2 through the pre-synthetic NGQDs. In other works, except for the pre-synthetic NGQDs, Ru@SiO2 had to be synthesized first.
The used reagents and apparatus, the synthesis process for GQDs and NGQDs were introduced in Supplementary Material.

The NGQDs-Ru@SiO2 self-enhanced ECL nanocomposites were synthesized through a sol-gel process according to the literature [39] with some modifications. First, 100 μL of 1.8 mg mL−1 NGQDs and 180 μL of 0.04 mol L−1 Ru(bpy)3 2+ were mixed while stirring for 12 h. The positive Ru(bpy)3 2+ absorbed the negative NGQDs. Then, 525 μL of cyclohexane, 125 μL of TX-100, and 125 μL of n-hexanol were added to the above mixture and mixed further for 30 min. Next, 50 μL of TEOS and 30 μL of NH3·H2O were added. After reacting for 24 h in the dark, the products were demulsified with acetone and centrifuged for 10 min at 12000 rpm. After washing with ethanol and ultrapure water thrice, the NGQDs-Ru@SiO2 self-enhanced ECL nanocomposites were obtained and dispersed in 1.0 mL of PBS solution. Ru@SiO2 was synthesized in a similar way without NGQDs.

The self-enhanced ECL nanocomposites-labeled peptide (peptide-NGQDs- Ru@ SiO2) were synthesized as shown in Scheme 1 . The peptide at 500 μL of 11.0 μmol L−1 was added to 500 μL NGQDs-Ru@SiO2 by incubation overnight at 4 °C. In this way, the peptide was covalently labeled onto the NGQDs-Ru@SiO2 through –NH2 on the peptide and –COOH on the NGQDs-Ru@SiO2 via acylation reaction. Finally, the above solution was placed in a dialysis bag (1000 Da), and 0.1 mol L−1 of PB (pH 7.4) was chose as the dialysis solution for 12 h. The self-enhanced ECL nanocomposites-labeled peptide nanoprobe was achieved and stored at 4 °C.

Before the experiment, a gold electrode was polished with 0.3 and 0.05 μm alumina slurry and was electrochemically cleaned in 0.10 mol L−1 H2SO4 with cyclic voltammetry for 20 cycles between +0.2 and + 1.5 V until a stable cyclic voltammogram was obtained. The self-enhanced ECL nanocomposites-labeled peptide biosensor was established as shown in Scheme 1. First, 10 μL of peptide-NGQDs-Ru@SiO2 was immobilized onto the cleaned gold electrode surface through Au–S bond for 3.5 h at room temperature and then thoroughly washed with PBS to remove the unbound ECL probe. Subsequently, the peptide-NGQDs-Ru@SiO2 modified electrode was immersed into of MCH (1.0 mmol L−1, 300 μL) to block the nonspecific binding sites for 30 min. After washing with PBS, the self-enhanced ECL nanocomposites-labeled peptide biosensor was obtained and stored in the refrigerator at 4 °C.

The self-enhanced ECL nanocomposites-labeled peptide biosensor was immersed into 100 μL of MMP-2 at different concentrations for 100 min at 37 °C. After careful washing and drying in air, the ECL signal was detected in 0.1 mol L−1 of PBS containing 50 mmol L−1 of TPA. The content of MMP-2 was measured by the change of ECL signal (△I = I
0
–I
S ), where I
0 and I
S were the ECL peak signal before and after the reaction of peptide-NGQDs-Ru@SiO2 biosensor with MMP-2, respectively.

The prepared NGQDs were characterized by TEM, XRD, UV and FL. The corresponding graphs and explanations were shown in Supplementary Material (Fig. S1). Fig. 1 A showed the morphology of the synthesized NGQDs-Ru@SiO2. The self-enhanced ECL nanocomposites consisted of numerous uniform nanospheres with an average size of 24 nm. As seen in Fig. 1B, the EDX spectrum confirmed C, N, O, Si, and Ru elements in NGQDs-Ru@SiO2, while presented the corresponding atomic contents of 20.01%, 12.33%, 55.01%, 11.51%, and 1.14%, respectively. From the TEM image in Fig. 1C, the NGQDs-Ru@SiO2 was evenly distributed, and the size was matched with the SEM results. Fig. 1D presented the fluorescence spectra of NGQDs-Ru@SiO2. The maximal emission was at 593 nm while the excitation at 464 nm, indicating the remarkable fluorescence property of NGQDs-Ru@SiO2. The inset was a photograph of NGQDs-Ru@SiO2. As depicted in Fig. 1E, the electric charges of Ru@SiO2 and NGQDs were −15.7 and −10.9 mV. However, the zeta potential of NGQDs-Ru@SiO2 changed to −27.3 mV. All these results proved that the synthesis of NGQDs-Ru@SiO2 was successful.

Fig. 1F displayed the FT-IR spectra of NGQDs and NGQDs-Ru@SiO2, respectively. The peaks at around 3426, 1645, and 1375 cm−1 were ascribed to υ (O–H and N–H), υ (C–H and CN), and υ (C = O and C = C), respectively [41]. Compared with NGQDs, new absorption peaks appeared at 1110 and 952 cm−1 in the FT-IR spectra of NGQDs-Ru@SiO2 can be assigned to υ (Si–O–Si) and υ (Si–O), respectively [42], demonstrating the presence of SiO2. Unfortunately, the absorption peak of the in-plane bending vibration peak of bipyridine at 1250 cm−1 was invisible, verifying the absence of Ru [43]. However, the EDX spectrum displayed the presence Ru. To gain insights into the existence of Ru in NGQDs-Ru@SiO2, XPS was performed. From the full spectra (Fig. 1G), Si 2p, Si 2s, C1s, and O1s peaks were detected. In addition, clear Ru 3d spectra was observed, suggesting the presence of Ru.

First, the Ru(bpy)3 2+/NGQDs ECL system property was studied (Fig. 2 A). Fig. 2A showed that Ru(bpy)3 2+ (0.1 mmol L−1) exhibited a weak ECL signal at 0.1 mol L−1 of PBS (pH = 7.0) when TPA was absent (803 a.u., line a). By comparison, the ECL intensity was improved to 1024 a.u. (line b) and 1257 a.u. (line c) when 0.1 mg mL−1 of GQDs and NGQDs existed, respectively. These results verified that NGQDs (or GQDs) acted as co-reactants, similar to the typical co-reactant of TPA and consistent with the literature report [17]. Moreover, Fig. 2A also illustrated that the sensitization of NGQDs was better than that of GQDs. This finding was possibly due to the N-doping-induced modulation of the chemical and electronic characteristics [44]. Considering the superiority of NGQDs, NGQDs was used to synthesize the self-enhanced nanocomposites instead of GQDs.

Next, the ECL properties of different nanoparticles were investigated in 0.1 mol L−1 of PBS (pH = 7.0) containing 50 mmol L−1 of TPA. As shown in Fig. 2B, a particularly weak ECL signal was found for NGQDs (line a, 716 a.u.), and Ru@SiO2 displayed relatively obvious ECL intensity (line b, 1550 a.u.). An ECL peak of approximately 2677 a.u. could be observed in the Ru@SiO2 + TPA + NGQDs solution (line c). Apparently, when NGQDs-Ru@SiO2 was introduced into the TPA solution, the ECL peak intensity was noticeably increased to 5812 a.u. (line d), which was approximately 2.2 times greater than curve c. This finding could be ascribed to the short distance between illuminant and co-reactant. The electron transfer efficiency was enhanced, and energy loss was reduced, thereby exhibiting a strong ECL response. It reasonably believed that NGQDs-Ru@SiO2 was successfully prepared and could be used as an ECL self-enhanced nanocomposite.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of ECL biosensor and feasibility study for MMP-2 detection
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 3A showed the CVs of different modified electrodes at 10 mmol L−1 of [Fe(CN)6]3-/4- (0.1 mol L−1 KCl). As depicted in Fig. 3 A, a pair of good reversible redox peaks at 0.308 and 0.225 V appeared in the bare gold electrode (line a). When ECL nanoprobe was self-assembled on gold electrode through Au–S covalent bond, the redox current increased (line b), because the nanoprobe was conductive and accelerated the electron transfer rate, thereby indicating that peptide-NGQDs-Ru@SiO2 had been successfully immobilized on the gold electrode surface. The redox current decreased when MCH was used to block the nonspecific binding sites (line c). In particular, when MMP-2 was immersed in ECL biosensor for 100 min, a further decrease in redox current was observed, thereby illustrating that the access of the redox of [Fe(CN)6]3-/4- was hindered (line d). This finding could be due to the fact that MMP-2 specifically cleaved the peptide (CGPLGVRGK) between G and V [40], which resulted in the leaving of a part of the peptide-NGQDs-Ru@SiO2 from the electrode surface. Thus, a decrease in electroactive substances was observed.

The feasibility for MMP-2 detection based on the ECL biosensor was clarified by using the ECL technique (Fig. 3C). The bare gold electrode has no ECL performance (line a). When peptide-NGQDs-Ru@SiO2 was linked onto the surface of the gold electrode via Au–S bond, a very strong ECL signal of 9533 a.u. was generated (line b), indicating that the synthesized self-enhanced ECL nanocomposites had strong ECL properties. Subsequently, MCH was used to block the non-specific sites, and due to its inferior conductivity, the ECL signal was reduced to 6748 a.u. (line c). In particular, at 10 and 150 ng mL−1, MMP-2 was immobilized on the ECL biosensor, and the ECL peak intensity decreased to 4158 a.u. (line d) and 1237 a.u. (line e), respectively. This finding was attributed to the fact that MMP-2 cleaved the peptide chain, causing a portion of peptide-NGQDs-Ru@SiO2 to leave the electrode surface. This finding indicated that a strategy based on self-enhanced ECL nanocomposites combined with the peptide could be applied to the detection of the target MMP-2.

The self-assembly time, cleavage time, peptide concentration and nanoprobe amount all have impact on the results of the experiment (Supplementary Material.). Finally, the self-assembly time was chosen as 3.5 h (Fig. S2a). 100 min was selected as the optimal cleavage time (Fig. S2b). Peptide concentration was set to 11.0 μmol L−1 (Fig. S2c). The amount of peptide-NGQDs-Ru@SiO2 was 13 μL (Fig. S2d).

To confirm the specificity of the constructed ECL biosensor, some substitutes were used, as follows: alpha fetoprotein (AFP), bovine serum albumin (BSA), prostate specific antigen (PSA), MMP-3, MMP-7, MMP-9, and MMP-10. As shown in Fig. 4B, the ECL signal did not noteworthy change compared with the blank signal, when the concentrations of potential interfering substances were 80 times more than the concentration of MMP-2. However, all these above mixtures containing 10 ng mL−1 MMP-2 showed an obvious ECL signal (4158 a.u.), which agreed with the MMP-2 solution (3919 a.u.) with a relative standard deviation (RSD) of 6.70%. The results illustrated that the peptide-based self-enhanced ECL biosensor can be realized for the specific detection of MMP-2 with high selectivity.

To investigate the reproducibility of the biosensor, 10 ng mL−1 of MMP-2 was measured using six different biosensors built simultaneously. The RSD was 5.31% (Fig. 4C, the blue bar charts), indicating good repeatability of the proposed ECL biosensor. Meanwhile, the stability of the established biosensor was studied after storage for 2 weeks at 4 °C. The results shown in Fig. 4C (the red bar charts) reveal a relatively stable detection of MMP-2.

To verify the applicability of the proposed biosensor, seven clinical serum samples (got from the Shangluo Hospital, ShaanXi, China) were diluted for 10 times and MMP-2 were detected according to experimental method. The results were in good agreement with that of enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA). The recovery was from 91.5% to 109.0% with a satisfactory RSD of 2.7 %–5.9% using the standard addition method, thereby indicating that the biosensor had good reliability (Table. S1).

In this research, new sensitive ECL self-enhanced nanocomposites of NGQDs-Ru@SiO2 were synthesized through a simple sol-gel reaction. The nanocomposites were used as signal substance, and the peptide was used as a recognition molecule. A novel biosensor of peptide-NGQDs-Ru@SiO2 for monitoring MMP-2 was prepared for the first time. Compared with other ECL biosensors, the established biosensor had a low detection limit. This finding was due to the fact that the ECL self-enhanced nanocomposites was prepared by using NGQDs as co-reactant. The process reduced the electron transfer distance and amplified the ECL strength. The proposed strategy could easily be extended to develop other ECL biosensors for other disease-associated biomarkers.

Xuemei Fan: Methodology, Writing – original draft. Shumin Wang: Investigation, Reviewing. Hugang Liu: Experiment. Zhejian Li: Experiment, Data curation. Qiangqiang Sun: Experiment, Software. Yimeng Wang: Writing – original draft preparation. Xinhui Fan: Design, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This project was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 30970696), the Science and Technology Planning Project of ShaanXi Province (2012KTDZ 02-02) and Science and technology innovation team project of shangluo university (18SCX003).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.talanta.2021.122830.

Bioelectron. 94 (2017) 694-700.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A novel strategy for improving amperometric biosensor sensitivity using dual-signal synergistic effect for ultrasensitive detection of matrix metalloproteinase-2

Chem. 86 (2014) 12398-12405.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Aqueous zymography screening of matrix metalloproteinase activity and inhibition based on colorimetric gold nanoparticles

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A graphene oxide-based fret sensor for rapid and sensitive detection of matrix metalloproteinase 2 in human serum sample

sb:title.sb:maintitle: One step electrochemical detection for matrix metalloproteinase 2 based on anodic stripping of silver nanoparticles mediated by host-guest interactions
